bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Exceeds FPA
bins.desc: The CPUC argues the ANOPR exceeds FERC authority under the FPA. Retail load energization is not a wholesale transaction, FPA section 212(h) bars retail wheeling mandates, and Commission precedent reserves such matters to states. Expanding federal jurisdiction risks reclassifying distribution assets as transmission and forcing retail unbundling.
quotes: the proposed rulemaking, as described in the ANOPR, would extend beyond FERC's jurisdiction and undermine the system of cooperative federalism set forth in the Federal Power Act and amendments in the Energy Policy Act of 2005 ("FPA").

The Commission and the states have separate zones of exclusive jurisdiction.  Critically, and key to the analysis here, the states retain authority over matters not expressly granted to the Commission under the FPA.
This type of indirect impact is beyond the scope of the Commission's jurisdiction under the Federal Power Act.

FPA section 212(h) prohibits FERC from directing the provision of transmission directly to end users (retail wheeling).  The FPA reservation of jurisdiction over retail rates and services to the states includes retail load energization.
The terms of large load energization are not a sale of electric energy at wholesale and therefore fall into the "any other" category reserved for the states.
expansion in Federal jurisdiction would lead to confusion and likely conflict between federal regulation and state-approved tariffs.

FERC has consistently recognized, including in Order Nos. 2003 and 2023, that interconnection service is distinct from transmission service.  Yet under the ANOPR's reasoning, the act of connecting a large retail customer directly to transmission facilities could transform that retail delivery into a FERC-jurisdictional wholesale transaction – redefining distribution facilities and costs as transmission assets, shifting oversight of distribution expansion, maintenance, and rate recovery from state commissions to FERC.

either this process would slow energization of large load and extend the timeline for bringing AI data centers online, or these questions would go unaddressed, resulting in unreasonable cost-shifting and a less reliable electric grid for all.

"Given the magnitude of the request and the importance of the issues implicated by that request, it would be patently unreasonable and bad public policy for FERC to use an expedited process to expand its jurisdiction."

bins.name: Flexible Guidelines Over Fixed Rules
bins.desc: If the Commission proceeds, the CPUC urges flexible guidelines rather than prescriptive rules. State and regional differences, evolving technology, and the diversity of large load customers mean that a one-size-fits-all rule would be counterproductive and slow energization.
quotes: flexibility and deference to states within their domains would benefit ratepayers, utilities, and industry alike.  Otherwise, the ANOPR's efforts may be counterproductive to the mutual goal of promptly energizing AI data centers.
flexible guidelines would be more effective than cumbersome rules.

"Any standardized procedures dealing with large load interconnection, including any efforts to address the costs of studying these customers, must be flexible enough to account for the evolving understanding of just what is necessary to integrate these customers to the grid."

bins.name: Voluntary Best Practices for Studies
bins.desc: The CPUC supports cooperative federalism through voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency and study procedures. Standardized study processes must remain flexible enough to evolve with grid integration needs.
quotes: "developing voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency, requiring load interconnection processes that enhance transparency, and other novel approaches" necessary at a particular moment.

"Any standardized procedures dealing with large load interconnection, including any efforts to address the costs of studying these customers, must be flexible enough to account for the evolving understanding of just what is necessary to integrate these customers to the grid."

bins.name: Cost Allocation Transparency
bins.desc: The CPUC supports public posting of cost responsibility determinations, annual consumer cost impact reports, and transparent methodology for cost causation. Costs from large load transmission upgrades should not be shifted to other ratepayers without clear, standardized criteria.
quotes: FERC has consistently recognized, including in Order Nos. 2003 and 2023, that interconnection service is distinct from transmission service.  Yet under the ANOPR's reasoning, the act of connecting a large retail customer directly to transmission facilities could transform that retail delivery into a FERC-jurisdictional wholesale transaction – redefining distribution facilities and costs as transmission assets, shifting oversight of distribution expansion, maintenance, and rate recovery from state commissions to FERC.

either this process would slow energization of large load and extend the timeline for bringing AI data centers online, or these questions would go unaddressed, resulting in unreasonable cost-shifting and a less reliable electric grid for all.
transparency measures "are necessary to protect residential consumers from bearing the costs of overbuilt transmission infrastructure resulting from inaccurate or inflated demand projections."

the Commission should require public utilities, Regional Transmission Organizations and Independent System Operators to provide public posting of:  1. All interconnection requests above [the MW threshold], including project type and location;  2. Cost responsibility determinations and any resulting upgrades; and   3. Annual reporting on the consumer cost impacts of large-load interconnections, associated transmission expansions, and the impact on electricity and capacity markets rates for consumers other than large loads.

Transparency in methodology for cost causation and cost allocation is critical to ensure the costs related to large load customers transmission network upgrades ("TNU's") are not unreasonably allocated in electricity rates to other customers.

bins.name: Higher MW Threshold With Non-MW Criteria
bins.desc: The CPUC shifts its position toward a higher MW threshold in response to Initial Comments showing broad variation across regions. It also supports defining the data center customer class by characteristics beyond MW alone, to protect local manufacturers and enable fair retail cost allocation.
quotes: the CPUC shifts its positions on the MW threshold towards a higher value and expands consideration of metrics for defining this large load customer class.

MW metrics alone may not be the defining feature of this type of load.  The CPUC agrees with the Ohio Office of Federal Energy Advocate ("Ohio FEA") that data centers are a unique customer class, and identifying them based on their exceptional characteristics is not unduly discriminatory.
if a final rule includes a nationwide MW threshold, the conservative next-best option is to set a threshold on the higher end of the spectrum of those proposed in Comments.

bins.name: Regulatory Stability for Investment
bins.desc: Perceived regulatory instability reduces investment. The CPUC warns that jurisdictional conflict between new federal rules and existing state-approved tariffs would undermine investment conditions and slow grid buildout.
quotes: expansion in Federal jurisdiction would lead to confusion and likely conflict between federal regulation and state-approved tariffs.

bins.name: Slower Process, Full NOPR Needed
bins.desc: The CPUC opposes rushing to final action by April 2026 and argues the ANOPR should lead to a full NOPR with adequate comment periods. An expedited process for a jurisdiction-expanding rule is unreasonable under 42 U.S.C. 7173(b) and the APA.
quotes: either this process would slow energization of large load and extend the timeline for bringing AI data centers online, or these questions would go unaddressed, resulting in unreasonable cost-shifting and a less reliable electric grid for all.

this ANOPR should be used to refine the proposals and prompts for a subsequent Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR"), providing sufficient time for input from utilities, grid operators, customers, and other stakeholder groups.

"Given the magnitude of the request and the importance of the issues implicated by that request, it would be patently unreasonable and bad public policy for FERC to use an expedited process to expand its jurisdiction."

bins.name: Public Queue Posting Requirements
bins.desc: The CPUC supports public posting of all large load interconnection requests, cost responsibility determinations, and annual consumer cost impact reports. Queue transparency prevents load shopping, queue gaming, and inflated demand projections that inflate wholesale rates and transmission costs.
quotes: transparency measures "are necessary to protect residential consumers from bearing the costs of overbuilt transmission infrastructure resulting from inaccurate or inflated demand projections."

the Commission should require public utilities, Regional Transmission Organizations and Independent System Operators to provide public posting of:  1. All interconnection requests above [the MW threshold], including project type and location;  2. Cost responsibility determinations and any resulting upgrades; and   3. Annual reporting on the consumer cost impacts of large-load interconnections, associated transmission expansions, and the impact on electricity and capacity markets rates for consumers other than large loads.

bins.name: Cooperative Federalism With States
bins.desc: The CPUC urges the Commission to build on state progress in large load energization through cooperative federalism rather than federal mandates. Voluntary best practices and deference to state regulatory frameworks will advance timely, reliable, and affordable energization.
quotes: "developing voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency, requiring load interconnection processes that enhance transparency, and other novel approaches" necessary at a particular moment.
